content:
Hey @Alexandre Courbot!
I would be interested in working on the numerical operations needed by Nova (https://docs.kernel.org/gpu/nova/core/todo.html#numerical-operations-numm).

Could you provide some more pointers for what is needed? For the case of the fls in C I saw it is optimized by using assembly for each architecture. Would we want to replicate that or provide a safe/convenient wrapper over the C function?

And what other operations are needed (if any)? I saw you already did some incredible work with the Bounded type!

Hey! I think we are mostly done with this task, a fls has been approved for upstream Rust, and we have basically all the infallible converters we needed in nova-core's num module. The last bit that remains is submitting these to the kernel crate, which I am about to resume doing

oh I see, so it's implemented in Rust directly. Well, good to hear! And thanks for the answer. Would you be able to recommend some work that somebody that isn't super familiar with Nova (yet!) can tackle?

Daniel del Castillo said:

oh I see, so it's implemented in Rust directly. Well, good to hear! And thanks for the answer. Would you be able to recommend some work that somebody that isn't super familiar with Nova (yet!) can tackle?

I'm also in search for that haha. My understanding is that it's a bit of a weird time where the work on the nova-core is almost done yet the work on the actual graphics part is not yet started. Can't do that without a proper hal.

So the advice I got is read the code and watch the discussions to see if extra work "pops up". There's also more general tasks in Rust for Linux that will be needed eventually in Nova. There's also the Tyr driver. There are some cool tasks if u have the hardware.
I see, thanks for the explanation! I'll try to keep an eye out then!
Indeed, I think R4L's good first issue list is a better fishing point at the moment if you want to get started. :slight_smile:
Also one thing that is always welcome is patch reviews with a Reviewed-by tag once think the code is ready. It's a good way to get familiar with the code and the inner workings of the hardware.
